title: Word Break
description:
You're given a string `s` and a dictionary of words `word_dict`. Determine whether `s` can be rebuilt by concatenating one or more words from the dictionary back to back, in any order, with each word reusable as many times as needed. Return `True` if such a segmentation exists, `False` otherwise.

**Example**

```
word_break("leetcode", ["leet", "code"])       -> True   ("leet" + "code")
word_break("applepenapple", ["apple", "pen"])  -> True   ("apple" + "pen" + "apple", reusing "apple")
word_break("catsandog", ["cats", "dog", "sand", "and", "cat"])  -> False
```
hints: Let dp[i] be True if the prefix s[:i] can be fully segmented into dictionary words; dp[0] = True (the empty prefix needs no words).
dp[i] is True if there is some earlier split point j < i where dp[j] is True and the substring s[j:i] is in the dictionary.
Words can be reused as many times as needed, so there's no bookkeeping to remove a word from the dictionary once it's used.

solutions.name: Brute-force recursion without memoization
solutions.explanation: From each starting index, try every possible next word boundary and recurse on the remainder; succeed if any branch reaches the end of the string. Without caching, overlapping subproblems (the same starting index reached via different splits) are recomputed repeatedly.
solutions.complexity: Time O(2^n) worst case, Space O(n) recursion depth

solutions.name: Bottom-up segmentation DP
solutions.explanation: Build dp[0..n] where dp[i] means the prefix of length i is segmentable. dp[0] is trivially True. For each end position i, scan every earlier split point j and set dp[i] True as soon as dp[j] is True and s[j:i] is a dictionary word. The final answer is dp[n].
solutions.complexity: Time O(n^2) (ignoring substring-hash cost), Space O(n)
